intro: The visible split is transparency: Linear scores 4/5, Cal.com scores 5/5. That changes how predictable each one feels at renewal time. Linear is built for saas and e-commerce teams managing user journeys and is not a fit for single-product apps with no ongoing user engagement. Cal.com is built for mobile and is not a fit for hobby projects. On the scoring side, Linear rates 5/5 for developer experience and 4/5 for transparency; Cal.com rates 5/5 and 5/5 respectively. The honest trade-off: with Linear you accept that contact-based pricing scales steeply with list growth, while Cal.com comes with the reality that manual deployment is sufficient. Neither side is free. Linear's headline strength in the data is that centralizes customer data and automates lifecycle messaging. Cal.com's headline strength is that automated build/test/deploy pipelines. Read each side's best-for and not-for fields carefully before you pick; the shape of your team and workflow matters more than any single score.
quick_take: Linear is for saas and e-commerce teams managing user journeys; Cal.com is for mobile; decide based on workflow fit.
verdict_a: Choose Linear if you are saas and e-commerce teams managing user journeys.
verdict_b: Choose Cal.com if you are mobile.

faq.a: Both list a freemium pricing model in our data, and both publish a free tier. We don't have per-seat numbers in this dataset, so 'cheaper' depends on volume. Price both on your actual usage before deciding.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Linear to Cal.com?
faq.a: Linear has low lock-in (issue tracker, data exportable); Cal.com has low lock-in (open-source, self-hostable, fully portable data). Migration effort scales with how much state and automation you've built in Linear; plan for data export, config rebuild, and a parallel-run period before cutting over.
Both Linear and Cal.com score 5/5 on developer experience in our data, so DX isn't the tiebreaker here. Decide on fit, pricing, or lock-in instead.

faq.q: Is Cal.com a good alternative to Linear?
faq.a: Cal.com is built for mobile and explicitly not for hobby projects, so it's a fit only if your workflow matches its stated audience. If you were using Linear for saas and e-commerce teams managing user journeys, the switch is reasonable; outside that scope, look elsewhere.
